coredata.dc:title: Intrinsic dual emissive insulin capped Au/Ag nanoclusters as single ratiometric nanoprobe for reversible detection of pH and temperature and cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Shamsipur, Mojtaba
Babaee, Elaheh
Gholivand, Mohammad-Bagher
Molaabasi, Fatemeh
Hajipour-Verdom, Behnam
Sedghi, Mosslim
coredata.dc:description: pH and temperature are two important characteristics in cells and the environment. These, not only in the well-done regulation of cell functions but also in diagnosis and treatment, have a key role. Protein-protected bimetallic nanoclusters are abundantly used in the building of biosensors. However, insulin-stabilized Au–Ag nanoclusters with dual intrinsic emission have not been investigated yet. In this work, Dual emissive insulin templated Au–Ag nanocluster (Ins(Au/Ag)NCs) were first synthesized in a simple and green one-put manner. The two emission wavelengths of, as-prepared NCs centered at 410 and 630 nm, excited in one excitation wavelength (330 nm). These two emission peaks were assigned to the di-Tyrosine cross-linked formation and bimetallic nanoclusters respectively. Further analysis displayed that each emission band of Ins(Au/Ag)NCs responded to one variable whilst another peak remained constant; For blue and red emission wavelengths, pH dependency and thermo-responsibility were observed respectively. As-prepared nanoprobe with the intrinsic dual emissive feature was used for ratiometric determination of these parameters, each with a discrete response from another. The linear range of 6.0–9.0 for pH and 1 to 71 °C for temperature was obtained, which comprises the physiological range of pH and temperature and afforded intracellular sensing and imaging capability. As-prepared NCs probe show excellent biocompatibility and cell membrane permeability, and so were successfully applied as direct ratiometric pH and temperature probes in HeLa and HFF cells. More interestingly, this dual emissive nanoprobe is capable of distinguishing cancer cells from normal ones.

A) Ratiometric Fluorescence emission spectra of the Ins(Au/Ag)NCs probe in different pH solution. B) The linear calibration range of (F410/F630) versus pH. C) The reversible change of ratiometric fluorescence intensity of the Ins(Au/Ag)NCs probe between pH value of 6 and 9. D) Fluorescence intensity ratio of the Ins(Au/Ag)NCs probe in PBS solution of pH 7.5 containing different concentrations of NaCl.
Fluorescence microscopic images of living Hela cells incubated with the dual emissive Ins(Au/Ag)NCs based probe in different pH solution. Original magnification were 10 × .

A) Ratiometric Fluorescence emission spectra of the Ins(Au/Ag)NCs probe at different temperature. B) The linear calibration range of (F630/F410) versus temperature. C) The reversible change of fluorescence intensity ratio of the Ins(Au/Ag)NCs probe between temperature of 1 °C and 50 °C. D) Arrhenius plot for Ins(Au/Ag)NCs.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis of Ins(Au/Ag)NCs and its application as pH and temperature nanoprobe.
Comparison of our nanoprobe (Ins(Au/Ag)NCs) with fluorescent nanoprobes that reported for simultaneous detection of pH and temperature.

row.entry: Need for pH-sensitive organic dye and sophisticated hybridization processes and a relatively limited range of temperature
Need for temperature-responsive copolymer ligand and a very limited range of temperature and pH (only is applicable in the acidic region)
The limited range of temperature and pH (only is applicable in the acidic region)
One signal simultaneously responds to two different variables
One signal simultaneously responds to two different variables, and artificial neural networks are used to solve this problem
Sophisticated synthesis with several processes for core-shell design and a very limited range of pH
Sophisticated synthesis with several processes for immobilization of several matters inside the silica nanoparticles as signal inducer and reference

Simple one-pot synthesis, an intrinsic response to pH and temperature, a wide range of temperatures that initiated from 1 that not reported by none of the previous works, and encompass the physiological pH.

head.ce:title: Intrinsic dual emissive insulin capped Au/Ag nanoclusters as single ratiometric nanoprobe for reversible detection of pH and temperature and cell imaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: pH and temperature are two important characteristics in cells and the environment. These, not only in the well-done regulation of cell functions but also in diagnosis and treatment, have a key role. Protein-protected bimetallic nanoclusters are abundantly used in the building of biosensors. However, insulin-stabilized Au–Ag nanoclusters with dual intrinsic emission have not been investigated yet. In this work, Dual emissive insulin templated Au–Ag nanocluster (Ins(Au/Ag)NCs) were first synthesized in a simple and green one-put manner. The two emission wavelengths of, as-prepared NCs centered at 410 and 630 nm, excited in one excitation wavelength (330 nm). These two emission peaks were assigned to the di-Tyrosine cross-linked formation and bimetallic nanoclusters respectively. Further analysis displayed that each emission band of Ins(Au/Ag)NCs responded to one variable whilst another peak remained constant; For blue and red emission wavelengths, pH dependency and thermo-responsibility were observed respectively. As-prepared nanoprobe with the intrinsic dual emissive feature was used for ratiometric determination of these parameters, each with a discrete response from another. The linear range of 6.0–9.0 for pH and 1 to 71 °C for temperature was obtained, which comprises the physiological range of pH and temperature and afforded intracellular sensing and imaging capability. As-prepared NCs probe show excellent biocompatibility and cell membrane permeability, and so were successfully applied as direct ratiometric pH and temperature probes in HeLa and HFF cells. More interestingly, this dual emissive nanoprobe is capable of distinguishing cancer cells from normal ones.

Application of Ins(Au/Ag)NCs as pH and temperature nanoprobe.
Application of Ins(Au/Ag)NCs as pH and temperature nanoprobe.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The pivotal role of pH and temperature in chemistry, biology, and medicine caused a regular investigation of these critical values. Most of the cellular functions show pH dependency; Proliferation and apoptosis, as well as enzyme activity and protein degradation, are examples of cellular actions regulated by intracellular pH to perform routine functions of cells. It is approved that, in some pathological conditions, including cancer, ischemia, inflammation, and infection, the extracellular pH reduces slightly (6.0–6.5) than normal ones (pH 7.2–7.4) (Han and Burgess, 2010; Thorarinsdottir et al., 2017). Moreover, a gradient of pH exists in the cells between early endosomes to late endosomes and lysosomes (Casey et al., 2010). Also, the temperature of living cells is measured as one of the parameters that govern biological reactions. Higher metabolic activities in pathological cells than normal ones are caused by a higher temperature in them (Song et al., 2007). Hence, drawing these variations in vitro and in vivo is crucial not only in the diagnosing process but also in treatment procedures.

In the last decade, smart probes that can simultaneously respond to more than one parameter have received much attention (Yue et al., 2019). These probes are desirable in various fields, such as biotechnology, drug delivery, and optical sensing to measure various chemical and biochemical analytes and parameters (Borisov et al., 2011; Stich et al., 2009). Analytes or parameters that have critical roles in the cell and tissue activity received more attention, for example, pH and temperature, whose abnormal values usually revealed an anomaly (Casey et al., 2010; Roos and Boron, 1981).

Fluorescence-based methods as “contactless” tools have been more desirable in the design of multi-analyte sensors and probes. Low background noise, high sensitivity, and fast response of the fluorescence method have introduced it as a powerful detection technique in chemistry and cell biology. Most preliminary fluorescence probes were single intensity-based, with output signals that were easily influenced by probe concentration, photobleaching, and even light source stability. Dual-emitting fluorescence probes for ratiometric measurement caused to circumvent these obstacles with built-in correction and upturn the sensitivity. On the other hand, the human eye in visual detection is more sensitive to changes in colors than brightness variation, which leads to the superiority of ratiometric fluorescence detection.

Traditionally, the combination of fluorescent dyes (Gao et al., 2007) or inorganic-organic hybrid (Chen et al., 2013b; Ding et al., 2015) have been used to construct multiple signals. But often, there are limitations such as requiring several excitation sources (Zhu et al., 2014b) and sophisticated filtering to reduce signal overlapping. These arise from broad emission bands and weak photostability of organic dyes (Yang et al., 2014) that, complicated the simultaneous analysis of multiple signals. Therefore, developing dual-emission fluorescent nanomaterials with high-performance optical properties, low toxicity, exact sensitivity, and simple operation is a key issue in the implementation of ratiometric fluorescent analyses.

Among various luminescent nanomaterial, metal nanoclusters (NCs) have recently received much interest. They have unique properties, such as high photostability and fluorescent efficiency, large stokes shifts, ultra-small size, facile synthesis, and especially lower toxicity than quantum dots (QDs) (Vosch et al., 2007; Zheng et al., 2007). The alloy NCs have been made later from the incorporation of heteroatoms into the monometallic NCs to enhance their photophysical properties. Typically, bimetallic nanoclusters exhibit higher luminescence intensity and stability than monometallic analogs (Bootharaju et al., 2016; Xie et al., 2018). Therefore, the alloying and doping strategy has been introduced to progress the NCs properties. Also, the synthesis of nanomaterials with intrinsic dual-emission wavelengths has received attention. Inherent dual emissive nanomaterials, especially metal nanoclusters, have simple synthesis and eliminate the time-consuming and sophisticated hybridizing process. Due to easy performance, dual emissive nanoclusters which excited in a single excitation wavelength are more attractive. So, diverse dual emissive gold, silver, or bimetallic nanoclusters with various stabilizing agents were synthesized and utilized as ratiometric probes for sensing different analytes (Cerretani and Vosch, 2019; Ding and Guo, 2015; Lei et al., 2016; Liu et al. 2016, 2018; Mo et al., 2019; Shu et al., 2018; Song et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2019; Zheng et al., 2017).

In this work, we firstly prepared bimetallic insulin capped Au/Ag nanoclusters with dual emission wavelengths in 410 and 630 nm, respectively. Interestingly, these dual emission bimetallic NCs responds independently to both temperature and pH value, and two emission wavelengths of this single probe distinguish two parameters. The blue emission of this probe responded to pH with a broad range that covers the biological pH value, and red emission remained unchanged. On the other hand, the temperature variation in an expansive window could vary the red emission wavelength of the synthesized probe while the blue emission remains constant. Therefore, this dual emissive proposed probe was used to ratiometric measurement of pH and temperature independently and also, was used as direct pH and temperature probes in HeLa and HFF cells.

Analytical grade tetrachloroauric(III) acid (HAuCl4), silver nitrate (AgNO3), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), hydrochloric acid (HCl), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium cyanide (NaCN), and all metal salts were purchased from Merck. Insulin of high purity was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Ultrapure water, 18.25 MΩ cm, was employed for all experiments.

Varian Cary Eclipse spectrofluorometer equipped with a xenon flash lamp as the excitation light source was employed for all fluorescence measurements. All measurements were performed in a 1 cm quartz cell. An Edinburgh Instrument FLS920 equipped with a xenon lamp as the excitation source (excitation wavelength 365 nm) was used to obtain fluorescence lifetime. Agilent 8453 diode array spectrophotometer and a Tensor 27Bruker instrument (Bruker, Japan), were used to record The UV–Vis and IR spectra respectively. All pH measurements were performed at 37.0 ± 0.1 °C by using a Metrohm 713 pH/ion-meter with a standard uncertainty of 0.1 mV (Metrohm, Switzerland). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded using a Zeiss-EM10C transmission electron microscope with an accelerating voltage of 80 kV. The XPS analyses were accomplished on an X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, 8025- BesTec twin anode XR3E2 X-ray source system).

All glassware was cleaned with freshly prepared aqua regia (HNO3/HCl, 1:3), subsequently rinsed with copious amounts of distilled water, and dried well before use. 4 mg of insulin was dissolved in 5 mL of ultrapure water in a glass beaker and 1 M NaOH solution was dropwise added to adjust the pH value to 10.5. After 10 min, 0.25 mL of AgNO3 (10 mM) was added to the solution and the reaction was allowed to proceed under vigorous stirring in the dark at 37 °C. After 24 h, 0.5 mL of HAuCl4 10 mM was added to the above solution and was allowed to stir for another 48 h to produce bimetallic Ins(Au/Ag)NCs. Finally, the solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min to remove any suspended particles and byproducts with large sizes, and the pale yellow solution of the as-prepared Ins(Au/Ag)NCs stored at 4 °C until required for further use.

In all experiments, 100 μl of Ins(Au/Ag)NCs solution was mixed with 900 μl PBS buffer and incubated for 5 min. After it, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded from 300 to 900 nm with an excitation wavelength of 330 nm. These measurements were repeated thrice for each pH detection and the average fluorescence signal was assigned. In addition, the pH cycling between pH 6.0 and 9.0 using acid and base as modulators were performed to evaluate the reversibility of the pH-sensitive as-prepared nanoprobe.

The solution of dual emissive Ins(Au/Ag)NCs (100 μl) incubated with 900 μL (10 mM) phosphate buffer solution pH7.4 at different temperature from 1 to 71 °C (at 5 °C intervals) for 15 min. After it, their fluorescence emission spectra were collected on the fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 330 nm and emission wavelength from 300 to 900 nm region. Furthermore, reversibility test for as-synthesized NCs thermoprobe examined by cycling the temperature between 1 °C and 50 °C.

Different concentrations of NaCl solution were mixed with PBS buffer (10 mM, pH 7.5) and 100 μl of the as-prepared NC probe solution. After 5 min incubation, the fluorescence was measured and recorded on the fluorescence spectrophotometer with an excitation wavelength of 330 nm and emission wavelength from 300 to 900 nm.

Cytotoxicity of as-synthesized dual emissive Ins(Au/Ag)NCs was evaluated on human cervical cancer HeLa cell line and human foreskin fibroblast (HFF) cells by MTT assay. The cells were obtained from the National Cell Bank of Iran (NCBI) at the Pasteur Institute of Iran. HeLa and HFF cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) that was supplemented with 10 % (v/v) heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS), 2 mM glutamine, 100 units/mL of penicillin and 100 mg/mL of streptomycin at 37 °C and 5 % CO2 in a humidified incubator. After that, cells were washed with 1X phosphate saline buffer (PBS), trypsinized (0.025 % trypsin, 0.02 % EDTA), seeded into 96-well cell culture plates (SPL Life Sciences Co., Ltd. Korea) at a density of 10 × 103 cells/well and incubated in 100 μL of supplemented media. After attaching overnight, the culture medium removed and cells were treated to fresh supplemented DMEM with 10 % FBS at different concentrations (0.05, 0.1, 0.25 and 0.5 mg/mL) of Ins(Au/Ag)NCs at 37 °C for 24 h. Then, the media was removed, and MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) solution (0.5 mg/ml in FBS-free DMEM) was added and incubated at 37 °C for an extra 4 h. After that, the media was discarded, and 100 μL DMSO was added into each well to dissolve MTT. Finally, the microplate reader (uQuant MQX200, BioTek, USA) was used to measure the optical density of each sample at 570 nm.

The HeLa and HFF cells (100 × 103 cells/well) were seeded into 6-well cell culture plates (SPL Life Sciences Co., Ltd. Korea), incubated in a humidified incubator containing 5 % CO2 at 37 °C, and allowed to attach overnight. For pH effect imaging, cells were incubated in the supplemented media at pH = 5.0, 7.4, and 9.0 for 12 h. After that, the medium was discarded, and cells were treated with 2 mg/mL of Ins(Au/Ag)NCs in FBS-free DMEM at the same pH for 4 h. Furthermore, for temperature effect imaging, Hela cells were treated with 2 mg/mL of Ins(Au/Ag)NCs in FBS-free DMEM at 25, 37, and 45 °C for 4 h. Finally, the cells were washed with PBS to remove the extracellular free compounds and followed by an additional 1 mL PBS before fluorescence imaging. The fluorescence images were collected by an Olympus IX-81 fluorescent microscope (Olympus Imaging System, Japan).

But, what happen in this synthesis that caused to produce new peak at 410 nm in addition to red fluorescence emission of bimetallic NCs? In our previous study, we synthesized the red emissive Au/Ag nanoclusters with insulin as protecting and reducing agents, the ratio of Au:Ag in the synthesis was 1:2. In this study we change the ratio to 2:1, and interestingly saw another emission peak in ∼410 nm in addition to the red emission wavelength in 630 nm. The effect of the molar ratio of metal ion precursors in the luminescence of dual emissive Au–Ag NCs has also been displayed previously (Ye and An, 2019). It has been proved that when insulin protein exposure to oxidative stress such as UV- light, H2O2, and etc., the absorbance of tyrosine (Tyr) residue at 276 nm decreases, and gradually a new absorption in ∼320 nm were introduced. On other hand, the emission corresponding to a tyrosine residue in 303 nm (Ex = 276) gradually decreased while a new emission band in ∼410 nm (maximum excitation in ∼320 nm) have appeared. These changes in absorbance, fluorescence emission, and excitation data are associated with di-Tyrosine cross-linked formation due to oxidation of protein (Correia et al., 2012; Su et al., 2015). Reduction of Au(III)/Ag(I) ions with Tyr residue caused to dityrosine formation (Shu et al., 2020).

On the other hand, due to similar excitation of 410 and 630 nm emission band (Fig. 1A) we calculate the radiative (kr) and nonradiative (knr) rates using the following equations to check the probability of energy transfer:

Whereas Φ and τ are the QY and the average lifetime of the sample. Based on average quantum yield calculated for di-Tyrosine which was calculated as 4.3 % in 410 nm by using quinine sulfate as a reference, and average lifetime measured at ambient condition as 63 ps for dual emissive nanoprobe, the kr and knr are 0.680 × 109 s−1 and 15.19 × 109 s−1 respectively. These results show that the energy transfer between di-Tyrosine cross-linked and (Au/Ag)NCs is nonradiative process. Based on previous report di-Tyrosine cross-linked has been well known as an energy transfer donor to the appropriate acceptors (Pu et al., 2011). Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) could be exist as radiationless energy transfer. For the investigation of FRET between di-Tyrosine cross-linked in Insulin and (Au/Ag)NCs in imprinted Insulin cages, chemical etching of the Insulin-protected (Au/Ag)NCs (Ins(Au/Ag)NCs) was chosen. It is proven that cyanide as a powerful etchant could remove the NCs from the protein cage and quench their fluorescence (Liu et al., 2010). As shown in Fig. S1with the increase of cyanide concentration, not only decrease the fluorescence intensity of (Au/Ag)NCs, but also increased the blue emission of di-Tyrosine cross-linked gradually. So, R

Based on the corresponding Förster relation and the above equations, we calculate J × 1013 as 6.45 M−1 cm−1, E as 0.110, R

Moreover, Su et al., (2015) reported the same fluorescence emission at 410 nm for BSA, lysozyme, and ovalbumin protected gold nanocluster after CSH-induced etching. This group, showed that blue emission spectra of di-Tyrosine cross-linking and absorbance of red emissive nanoclusters overlapped, and due to quenching mechanisms of inner filter effect (IFE) only fluorescence of gold nanoclusters had been observed. Su and coworkers (Shu et al., 2020) reported that dilution could circumvent this IFE of AuNCs on di-Tyrosine residues. As could be seen from Fig. 1A, due to proper dilution of our as-prepared solution however the excitation band of red emission (centered at 330 nm) and blue emission fluorescence spectra (centered at 410) overlapped with him but both blue and red emission could be observed simultaneously to have dual emissive probe. Likewise, they show that in the presence of IFE the dilution of as-synthesized dual emissive probe caused to gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity of di-Tyrosine and MNCs (Shu et al., 2016). Hence we examine the effect of dilution and interestingly a similar result was observed (Fig. S2). This result shows the obvious change of the 630 nm emission with dilution of the solution, which proves the existence of IFE as the second mechanism of excitation of (Au/Ag)NCs. Accordingly, based on the shoulder of the excitation spectrum recorded at 630 nm observed in Fig. 1A, the Ins(Au/Ag)NCs not only excited by direct absorption of the excitation light at 330 nm but also indirectly by FRET and/or by direct absorption of the 410 nm emission that tack place in IFE.

Other characterization methods including FTIR spectroscopy, XPS, TEM, and zeta potential, in addition to the photoluminescence spectroscopy employed to elucidate the dual emissive Ins(Au/Ag)NCs. The quantum yield of this NCs was calculated to be 7.1 % in 630 nm by using Rhodamine B as reference. It could be concluded that synergistic effect of Ag in spherical morphologies of Ins(Au/Ag)NCs clearly observed from HRTEM images (Fig. 1B); Using the 100 particles in the images the size of the bimetallic Ins(Au/Ag)NCs were measured and the average diameter of 1.42 ± 0.38 nm was calculated. The inset of Fig. 1B show that, the lattice fringe structure of AuNCs and AgNCs could be clearly observed (Li et al., 2023). A large excess of electrostatic repulsion between particles caused to well dispersion in aqueous solutions and could be realized from the zeta potential of −30.4 ± 6 mV.

The FTIR spectra of the insulin protein and the as-synthesized dual emissive bimetallic NCs were shown in Fig. S3. Comparison of these two curves show that, intensity of almost all peaks were changed after synthesis process which could be associated to the formation of Insulin-capped NCs. CO stretching vibration peaks at region 1600-1700 cm−1 attributed to the amide I and C–N stretching and N–H bending vibrations peaks at 1550 cm−1 correspond to amide II. As see from FTIR spectra, these mentioned peaks are approximately constant with no shift before and after synthesis that could be associated to the constant structural conformation of secondary structure of the insulin protein. Likewise, the absence of free S–H bonds in the structures of Insulin and Ins(Au/Ag)NCs could be confirmed by the lack of any peak at the 2550 cm−1 region in its FTIR spectra that is due to S–S linkage in insulin protein. So, it could be concluded that the encapsulation of the Ins(Au/Ag)NCs does not occur through the Au–S or Ag–S bonds (Shamsipur et al., 2023).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) employed to explore the valence and approximate composition of the as-synthesized Ins(Au/Ag)NCs (Fig. 1C and D), which demonstrate both Au and Ag atoms exist in composition with Au(0), Au(І), Ag(0) and Ag(І) valence. Each of Au4f7/2 (84.38 eV),Au4f5/2 (87.88 eV) spectra (Fig. 1C) and Ag3d3/2 (367.58 eV), Ag3d5/2 (373.68 eV) spectra (Fig. 1D) were de-convoluted into two distinct peaks correspond to Au(0) and Au(I) or Ag(0) and Ag(І) respectively, that approves the more fraction of Au(0) and Ag(0) in composition. Besides, based on our XPS results and previous reports (Sun et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2014), we speculated that bimetallic Ins(Au/Ag)NCs were synthesized versus core-shell Au@AgNCs due to the presence of Au(0) with more fraction in composition.

The effect of pH and temperature on the fluorescence intensity of synthesized dual emissive NCs was checked out, interestingly showing the emission wavelength at 410 nm responded to pH while 630 nm remained constant at different pHs (Scheme 1). On the other hand, changing the temperature only affects the emission wavelength at 630 nm and the other one shows no alteration. So, this intrinsic dual emissive bimetallic NCs served as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for simultaneous determination of pH and temperature. In single intensity based probes, inaccurate quantification could be occurring due to instability in a single fluorescence signal that arises from changes in environmental conditions, variation in probe concentrations, fluctuation in the light source, and photobleaching. These deficiencies could be overcome by ratiometric probes.

Due to sophisticated design and time consuming synthesis procedure of traditionally dual emissive probes developed by the conjugation of different single emissive fluorophores, intrinsic dual emissive nanomaterials with two distinct wavelength emissions are more attractive. Recently, dual emissive metal nanoclusters which stabilized with different proteins (Ding and Guo, 2015; Li and Ding, 2015; Liu et al., 2016; Mo et al., 2019; Shu et al., 2018; Zhuang et al., 2014), amino acids (Liu et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2019; Zheng et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2014a), DNA (Wang et al., 2019) and other ligand (Lei et al., 2016; Xu et al., 2015) have been synthesized and employed as ratiometric probe for target analytes. Most of these intrinsic dual emissive NCs were synthesized with BSA and Lysozyme proteins as protecting agents, but until now dual emissive insulin scaffold nanoclusters have not been synthesized. Moreover, the majority of these intrinsic dual emissive NCs were employed to determine only one analyte or factor and up to now, simultaneous ratiometric detection of environmental pH and temperature changes with intrinsic dual emissive NCs have not been reported.

Quantitative determination of environmental pH is one critical adjustment in many chemical and biological processes. As mentioned above, the emission wavelengths of as-synthesized dual emissive Ins(Au/Ag)NCs in 410 and 630 nm, show a pH dependency and independency characteristic respectively (Fig. S4). As shown, the pH ranging from 4.0 to 11.0, was applied and the fluorescence emission spectra were collected. The location of fluorescence emission wavelength maxima at 410 nm remained constant, though the emission intensity of it with the increase of the pH (4.00–11.00) raised gradually. In all records, the Ins(Au/Ag)NCs probe excited at 330 nm, and the emission ratio of (F
410
/F
630 ) was calculated and served as the dependent variable. All experiments were carried out at a fixed temperature of 37 °C.

Linear response of (F
410
/F
630
) versus pH was obtained in the range of 6.0–9.0 (Fig. 2 A), based on the following equation: (F
410
/F
630 ) = 0.7946 pH - 4.4972, with R2 = 0.9909, which indicated a wide dynamic range that encompass the physiological pH (Fig. 2B). The dynamic range of as-prepared probe is more comprehensive than that provided by commercial products such as LysoSensor Yellow/Blue which only work in the acidic region.

One critical characteristic of a fluorescent pH probe is reversibility, which provides the monitoring of the reversible change of the pH value of the organism. So, the pH value of the aqueous solution of the Ins(Au/Ag)NCs quickly changed by using concentrated aqueous sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid, then the fluorescence intensity values in acidic (pH = 6.0) and basic (pH = 9.0) conditions were collected. As shown in Fig. 2C, the pH value is switched back and forth between 6.0 and 9.0, as well as the fluorescence intensity values. The response and recovery times are within seconds and after four cycles, there is still up to 99.9 % of the fluorescence intensity successfully recovered in acidic and basic conditions. It could be clearly concluded that dual emissive Ins(Au/Ag)NCs exhibit a highly reversible response to pH. Furthermore, five repeated measurements of pH = 8 in five solutions were performed and an RSD of 1.2 % was calculated. Also, the effect of NaCl concentration on the fluorescence intensity of Ins(Au/Ag)NCs were carried out, and no significant changes were observed even for a high NaCl concentration of 1.0 M (Fig. 2D). After the addition of 1.0 M concentration of NaCl to the solution, the fluorescence intensity ratio was only reduced by 4.57 percent. This result suggests that Ins(Au/Ag)NCs have great potential for sensing applications under physiological conditions.

As mentioned previously, the blue fluorescence emission (410 nm) was assigned to the dityrosine cross-link that was increased by growing the ratio of gold ions precursor. Based on the phenolic structure of dityrosine cross-linked (Fig. S5) it could be concluded that upon increasing the pH of the solution, deprotonation of hydroxyl groups occurred. Plus, according to the previous reports among phenolic protons of dityrosine only ionization of one of them take place (Andersen, 1964). Analysis of the Excitation of 410 nm emission in as-synthesized NCs (∼330 nm) as a function of pH, revealed that maximum absorbance in acidic solution shifts to a lower wavelength of ∼290 nm (Fig. S6). Which increasing the pH caused to red shift and appearance in upper wavelength (∼325 nm). This pH dependency of absorbance, due to deprotonation of phenolic hydroxyl groups of dityrosine, reflected in greater fluorescence intensity of dityrosine cross-linked in 410 nm with increasing the pH. These observations were in accordance with previous reports (Mahmoud and Bialkowski, 1995; Su et al., 2015). Also, as we proved in our previous work (Shamsipur et al., 2023) when the ligand-to-metal charge transfer is responsible for the luminescence, is affected by the change in the surrounding solution environment, while in this study the red emission of Ins(Au/Ag)NCs remained constant in different pH and so could show the transition of electrons between different energy levels of the metal core.

This new synthesized NCs hold great potential as a dual-emission probe for ratiometric determination of local pH values due to significantly different emission wavelengths (410 and 630 nm), and the same excitation wavelength (330 nm). The cytotoxicity of dual emissive NCs was investigated by MTT assay prior to using probe for cell imaging. Obtained results shown in Fig. S7 for Hela and HFF cells that revealed as-prepared bimetallic NCs do not show significant toxicity up to 0.1 mM concentration. Thus, this Ins(Au/Ag)NCs could be appropriately used for in vitro imaging.

The cells used for this study were suspension cultures of a strain of HeLa cells treated with acidic and basic PBS solutions. As we can see in Fig. 3 , the color intensity of blue fluorescence, corresponding to the emission at 410 nm in cells, enhanced upon increasing the pH values from 5.0 to 9.0. While the red fluorescence intensity (650 nm emission) was almost constant in all these cell images. The merge of these images represents a gradual increase in purple color with the increasing pH (Fig. 3).

Interestingly, the Ins(Au/Ag)NCs were located in the cytosol inside the cells. On the other hand, HFF cells were used as the normal cell line and after being treated with different pH solutions the images were collected. Fig. S8, display these images and show that in different pH conditions significant change did not occur in these cells. One can conclude that the proposed ratiometric pH probe is a simple, cell-permeable, non-toxic, and convenient probe for local pH imaging in cancer cells and thus exhibits a potent functional system for detection and tracing methods to distinguish normal cells from cancer cells. This distinction could be a result of the different physiological conditions between cancer cells and normal cells, particularly CD44 receptor abundance. It has been specified that the normal human foreskin fibroblasts (HFF) and human cervix cancer (HeLa) cells have low and high CD44 expression respectively. These CD44 receptors would facilitate the nanoprobe uptake (Robby et al., 2021).

Additional studies show the effect of temperature on fluorescence intensity of dual emissive Ins(Au/Ag)NCs. While, the red and blue fluorescence emissions represent the temperature-dependency and independence features, respectively. From Fig. 4 A, when the temperature rises from 1 to 71 °C, the fluorescence intensity of as-prepared NCs at 630 nm gradually drops while the emission intensity at 410 nm is almost constant. This large range of temperature dependency, seen for the synthesized Ins(Au/Ag)NCs, embraces the physiological temperature and provides potential applicability to cell imaging and in vivo temperature sensing.

The constancy of peak positions during the change of temperature displays the stability and high purity of synthesized thermo-responsive NCs probe (Huang et al., 2014). This is while the temperature-sensitive quantum dots commonly show the spectral shift (Maestro et al., 2011). It seems the enhancement of nonradiative decay induced by temperature caused the fluorescence quenching (Chen et al., 2013a; Zhou et al., 2015). We investigated the fluorescence lifetime of Ins(Au/Ag)NCs at two different temperatures to prove this. The results show that when the temperature is reduced from 30 °C to 10 °C, the average lifetime increases from 63 ps to 119 ps (Fig. S9). The temperature enhancement cause increases in the nonradiative transition rate and molecular collision frequency, while the radiative transition rate remains constant, so the fluorescence intensity reduces. On other hand, such a short lifetime (ps) that resulted for Ins(Au/Ag)NCs, could be related to the transition of electrons between different energy levels of the metal core that caused the luminescence in Ins(Au/Ag)NCs.

These intrinsic dual emission features of as-prepared NCs enable us to the ratiometric measurement of temperature. In which blue emission intensity at 410 nm was considered as reference and all data normalized to it. As shown in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F
630
/F
410 ) value of dual emissive Ins(Au/Ag)NCs probe decreased linearly (R
2 = 0.9919) as the temperature increased from 1 to 71 °C. Upon increasing the temperature in this temperature region (F
630
/F
410 ) signal falling-off to 26 % of its initial value with a sensitivity of −1.23 % °C−1. Compared with previously reported metal NCs-based thermometers, this sensitivity of response for the as-prepared probe is higher than most of them (Chen et al., 2014; Huang et al., 2014; Shang et al., 2013). As regards to the pH dependency of as-prepared dual emissive NCs, all temperature measurement carried out in constant pH 7.4 (PBS 10 mM). The reversibility feature was checked between 1 °C and 50 °C for four consecutive cycles, and any exhaustion in the temperature range was not observed (Fig. 4C). After four cycles, Up to 97.2 % of original fluorescence emission intensity comes back. That means, Ins(Au/Ag)NCs probe owing to its wide linear temperature detection range and good reversibility could be applicable as a reliable temperature probe as well as a practical pH probe.

The activation energy of Ins(Au/Ag)NCs was estimated using the following equation:
(6)
F o
F
= 1 + A e x p
(
− E
a
k B
T
)
Where F
0 and F correspond to the fluorescence intensities (at 630 nm wavelength) at the initial and varied temperatures, the pre-exponential factor A is a dimensionless constant, E
a is the activation energy in the thermal quenching process, k
B is Boltzmann's constant, and T is the absolute temperature. On plotting the natural logarithm of (F
0
/F − 1) against 1/k
B
T, the activation energy of Ins(Au/Ag)NCs was derived as 179.2 meV (Fig. 4D). Comparison of our result with previously reported activation energy obtained for fluorescent thermometers, revealed that our obtained value is larger than most of these reported values (Chen et al., 2013a; Valerini et al., 2005; Wu et al., 2016; Yu et al. 2012a, 2012b). It could be concluded that our offered probe is more sensitive to variation of temperature than other nanomaterial-based thermometer such as carbon dots and quantum dots. Correspondingly, the activation energy of the non-radiative processes for the 410 nm emission intensity was calculated, and it was seen that the activation energy of di-Tyrosine was derived as 0.01 μeV which shows the invariance of the 410 nm band intensity with temperature (Fig. S10). These results confirmed the benefit of ratiometric thermometry rather than single intensity-based measurements. Accordingly, advantages including no spectral shifts, well activation energy, and reusability potential suggested the Ins(Au/Ag)NCs probe as a valid self-calibrated fluorescent thermometer.

As mentioned before, IFE could exist between the di-Tyrosine and Ins(Au/Ag)NCs and so due to the constancy of the maximum absorption wavelength of Ins(Au/Ag)NCs and the maximum emission wavelength of di-tyrosine in different temperatures and pH, we could conclude that di-Tyrosine emission only affected by pH change and temperature change could only variate the Ins(Au/Ag)NCs emission. And due to proper dilution of the solution, a negligible spectral overlap of di-Tyrosine cross-linked emission and Ins(Au/Ag)NCs excitation spectrum exist that are not affected significantly by changing the pH and temperature.

Moreover, In single intensity-based measurements of temperature the probability of attendance of quenching by oxygen, nanoprobe aggregation, and temperature-induced reduction, is caused by the non-specificity of the probe (Han et al., 2009; Löw et al., 2008). In this regard, the single fluorescence intensity of Ins(Au/Ag)NCs at 630 nm without normalizing to blue emission was recorded as a function of temperature alteration, and we saw that a poor linear relationship (R
2 = 0.9399) was obtained (Fig. S11).

Similar to pH detection, the response of dual emissive nanoclusters probe to different temperature in Hela cell (constant pH7.4) were performed and results are shown in Fig. S12. Although the red emission of dual emissive nanoclusters gradually decreased with temperature enhancement, due to the weaker blue emission band of di-Tyrosine crosslinked in this pH, blue emission could not be seen clearly.

Although many different probes have reported measurements of pH or temperature in articles, up to now only very scarce examples of simultaneous determinations of them have been presented. Based on this, great efforts have been paid to the preparation of dual probes for the determination of pH and temperature synchronously. Traditional dual pH and temperature probes were made from the different indicators incorporated in one matrix, such as polymer/hydrogel and silicon (Borisov et al., 2011; Hu et al., 2011; Stich et al., 2009; Wan and Liu, 2011). Need for different fluorophore substances with suitable emission and excitation wavelengths that especially answered these critical factors and the necessity to have the ability to integrate together make the synthesis processes complex and time-consuming.

Recently, different fluorescent nanomaterial include nanoclusters (Lü et al., 2018; Sun et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2019), carbon dots (Sarmanova et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2015), hydrogel nanoparticles (Zhao et al., 2016), and metal-organic framework (Ding et al., 2019) employed to construct dual probes for these critical factors. It can be seen that there are only a few nanoprobe reports available for this simultaneous determination. Comparison of our proposed nanoprobe with other simultaneous pH and temperature determination sensor works were listed in Table 1 . As could be seen from this, some nanoprobe are ratiometric but response of them are not intrinsic and one pH or temperature indicator combined with nanomaterial to construct a dual emission dual-probe. This arrangement processes have hardest the work. On another hand, some of these dual-probes are single intensity based and constructed from one nanofluorophore that is sensitive to all two features. In these, the fluctuations of various parameters in solution in addition to the alteration of pH and temperature affected the signal and not only the simultaneous determination is impossible, but also other environmental parameters inadequate the probe sensitivity and selectivity.

Compared with the previously reported nanoprobes, our proposed probe have suitable and expansive windows of pH and temperature that make it favorable to use in the bioanalytical purpose. On the other hand, the simple one-pot and green synthesis manner and the intrinsic dual emission band with a single excitation wavelength distinguishes our suggested nanoprobe from other like it. More intriguingly, each emission band of our nanoprobe responded to one of these parameters separately and independently, and the determination of both of these certain features at one time has become possible. Based on the best of our knowledge, one intrinsic multiple emissive nanoflourophore that responds to both temperature and pH has not yet been reported.

In summary, a facile, straightforward, green, and one-put method was reported to prepare water-soluble and highly luminescent Au/Ag nanocluster using insulin as both protecting and reducing agent in alkaline conditions. As-synthesized nanoclusters show intrinsic dual emission bands with distinct wavelength emissions and one excitation wavelength. Interestingly, blue and red emissions bands respectively display pH and temperature dependency independently. These inherent features make it applicable for ratiometric and simultaneous determination of pH and temperature. So, a nanoclusters-based probe was constructed for ratiometric and simultaneous determination of these two crucial physiological and biological parameters with wide-range linear response. Consequently, as-prepared NCs based probe was applied to recognize local pH values inside HeLa and HFF cells and show the capability for distinguishing cancer cells from normal ones.
